title: Welsh rarebit
subtitle: A proper grown-up cheese on toast.
excerpt: Mature cheddar melted with butter, flour, ale, English mustard, and Worcestershire into a thick sauce, spread on toast and grilled until bubbled and gold. Twenty minutes start to finish, the savoury supper at the heart of British pub food.
sourceNotes: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), public domain, gives an early form as 'Welch rabbit'. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), settles the modern roux-based version with ale.

recipe.freezeNotes: Made-up rarebit sauce freezes for a month in a small tub; defrost in the fridge, beat smooth with a teaspoon of milk, spread on toast.
recipe.batchNotes: The sauce doubles or triples without trouble. The grilling is the rate-limiting step.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sauce keeps three days in the fridge; let it come to room temperature and beat smooth before spreading.
recipe.temperatureNote: all done under the grill on its highest setting

glossaryTerms.definition: A short butter-and-flour roux cooked briefly, into which milk, ale, or a mix is whisked, then cheese melted at the lowest heat. The flour stabilises the cheese so it spreads and grills without splitting into oil and protein.

content.text: Two slices of sourdough toasted gold, topped with a spoonful of thick cheese sauce built on a butter roux with ale, English mustard, and a slug of Worcestershire. Slid back under a screaming hot grill until the surface puffs, blisters, and browns to caramel patches. Twenty minutes from cold hob to plate.

The English name 'rabbit' was a joke about food rarely seen on the table; over time the spelling shifted to 'rarebit' to sound more decorous. Mature cheddar is essential — supermarket mild cheese makes wet, bland rarebit. Get a strong block, grate it generously, and don't skimp on the mustard.

items.prepNote: or half a teaspoon of English mustard powder

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small saucepan over a low hob. Whisk in {{plain-flour}} of plain flour; cook for one minute, whisking, until the paste smells of biscuits. The
Pour {{beer}} of ale into the pan in three stages, whisking smooth between each. The sauce thickens and the alcohol burns off. Cook for two minutes.

Take the pan off the hob. Stir in {{cheddar}} of grated mature cheddar a handful at a time, letting each handful melt fully before adding the next. The sauce drops to thick paste. Stir in {{english-mustard}} of English mustard, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire, and a generous twist of black pepper. Beat in {{egg-yolks}} egg yolk if you want extra silk.
Toast the bread, top the toast
Toast {{bread}} slices of sourdough to deep gold. Sit them on a baking tray. Spread half the rarebit mixture across each slice, generous and slightly mounded at the centre.

Slide the tray under a hot grill, 10 cm from the heat. Grill for 3 to 4 minutes, watching closely, until the top is puffed, blistered, and gold-brown with darker patches. Eat immediately — the sauce stiffens as it cools.

items.cause: Cheese melted over too high a heat.
items.fix: Take the pan off the hob before any cheese goes in. The residual warmth melts; direct heat splits.

items.cause: Sauce too thin from too much liquid.
items.fix: Add cheese in stages until the paste holds a peak on the spoon. Reduce a thin sauce briefly over low heat.

items.cause: Grill not hot enough, or too far from the heat.
items.fix: Highest setting, 10 cm away, 3 to 4 minutes. Watch every second.
Mature cheddar; full teaspoon of English mustard at minimum.

items.cause: Toast not dark enough before the sauce went on.
items.fix: Toast to deep gold first. A pale soft toast can't hold the weight.

content.text: Rarebit belongs to the British supper canon — what was once called a 'savoury', served after the sweet at a Victorian dinner table or eaten on its own as a late kitchen snack. Add a fried egg on top and it becomes a buck rarebit; pour over a wedge of grilled tomato and it becomes a blushing bunny. A glass of strong ale or a cup of properly stewed tea.
